key_departures.impact: Oweh was acquired via midseason trade from Baltimore and exploded for 7.5 sacks in 12 regular season games plus a franchise-record 3 sacks and 2 forced fumbles in the playoff loss to New England. Signed a 4-year, $100M deal ($68M guaranteed) with Washington. His departure removes the Chargers' most disruptive pass rusher and second-best edge option behind Mack.

The 2022 first-round pick (No. 17 overall) was the Chargers' starting left guard all four seasons. Signed a 3-year, $49.5M deal ($32.4M guaranteed) with Cleveland. The Chargers declined to match. His departure, combined with the release of Mekhi Becton and Bradley Bozeman's retirement, leaves a massive IOL void — effectively all three interior starters gone.

Starting center for the past two seasons retired in February 2026. He graded last among PFF's qualified centers in 2025. Left no center under contract on the roster. Departure creates a critical need — but was addressed quickly with the Biadasz signing.

Released March 4, saving just under $10M in cap space. Becton struggled with nagging injuries and inconsistent play in his lone season with the Chargers. Departure eliminates the starting right guard, adding another interior line hole on top of the Johnson departure and Bozeman retirement.

St-Juste played only 37% of defensive snaps but posted the 11th-best coverage grade among 114 qualified CBs per PFF. Signed a 2-year, $10M deal with Green Bay — a value the Chargers chose not to match. His departure removes important CB depth behind starters Tarheeb Still and Cam Hart.

Signed a 1-year, $3M deal with Dallas. Was a rotational piece behind Teair Tart for four seasons. Depth loss at IDL, but with Tart re-signed on a 3-year deal and Jamaree Caldwell under contract, the impact is minor.

Harris missed most of 2025 with an Achilles injury and hit free agency. His departure removes veteran RB depth but the Chargers have Omarion Hampton, Kimani Vidal (tendered), and added Keaton Mitchell.

signings.analysis: Signed January 26 before free agency opened — the Chargers' first and most important pre-FA move. Tart's 3-year, $30M deal with $20M guaranteed locks up the Chargers' best interior defender, who ranked 5th in PFF run defense grade (74.7) among linemen with 240+ snaps in 2025. At $10M AAV, this is mid-tier starter money that represents strong value for a player of this caliber. IDL is fully addressed — Tart anchors the position for three years with Jamaree Caldwell as depth.

One-year, $18M fully guaranteed — the same structure as last year. Mack is a future Hall of Famer but turns 35 in 2026 and was limited to 12 games with 5.5 sacks in 2025 due to an elbow injury. At $18M AAV for a one-year deal, this is solidly in the mid-tier range for the position but reflects his age and injury history. His return alongside Tuli Tuipulotu (13.5 sacks in 2025, playing out his rookie deal) gives the Chargers a functional starting duo. However, the loss of Oweh and the one-year bridge nature of Mack's deal means EDGE is still a real concern — they need a third and long-term starter option.

Signed March 6 (before the league year opened) after Washington released him. 3-year, $30M deal with $20M guaranteed — mid-tier starter value at center. Biadasz graded 15th in PBWR and 16th in RBWR among centers in 2025 per ESPN, a significant upgrade from the retired Bozeman (who ranked 30th in PBWR). He fills the center position but the Chargers still have gaping holes at both guard spots (lost Zion Johnson, cut Becton). IOL remains a high-priority need despite this signing.

2-year, $13M deal ($9M guaranteed) reuniting him with OC Mike McDaniel, under whom he started 14 games at right guard in Miami in 2025. At $6.5M AAV, this is below the mid-tier starter threshold for guards. Strange allowed 21 pressures and 16 hurries in Miami — functional but not reliable pass-blocking. The contract structure (light guarantees, low AAV) signals a potential starter but not a long-term solution. The Chargers will almost certainly draft a guard. Minimally addresses the RG need — it remains a premium draft target.

1-year, $4.5M extension. Penning is a swing tackle/guard who can compete for a starting guard role. The Chargers have starters Rashawn Slater and Joe Alt returning from injury at tackle, so Penning slots as OL depth and emergency guard competition. Depth signing with some upside if he develops, but does not move the needle on either the OT or IOL need.

2-year, $10M extension. Pipkins held down RT after Slater's season-ending injury in 2025 and was one of the Chargers' more consistent linemen on a historically bad OL. His return provides the swing tackle insurance the Chargers need with two franchise tackles coming back from injury. Pure depth signing that correctly slots behind Slater and Alt. Could also compete for a guard start. Does not address a need, but provides necessary roster infrastructure.

3-year, $24.3M deal ($17M guaranteed) — mid-tier starter money for a TE who was third on Baltimore's depth chart behind Andrews and Likely but caught 10 passes for 142 yards and 2 TDs in 2025. The $17M in guarantees signals real commitment. Kolar is an elite blocking TE — the highest-paid blocking TE in the NFL per OC Register — that fits Harbaugh/McDaniel's run-heavy identity. However, the Chargers released Will Dissly and were nearly empty at TE before this. Kolar pairs with Oronde Gadsden to create a functional two-TE setup. TE is partially addressed — Gadsden/Kolar is workable but a pass-catching TE upgrade via draft remains on the table (per Kenyon Sadiq buzz at No. 22).

2-year, $7.5M deal for the Pro Bowl fullback who reunites with OC Mike McDaniel after four seasons together in Miami. Ingold is a position unto himself (FB) and directly enables McDaniel's run-heavy scheme alongside Omarion Hampton and Kimani Vidal. His signing likely ends Scott Matlock's two-way role (FB/DT). Does not address a positional need per se, but is a meaningful offensive addition that enhances the run game infrastructure.

2-year, $9.25M deal ($5M guaranteed). Mitchell replaces the departed Najee Harris as the third-down/change-of-pace back behind Hampton and Vidal. His 4.37 speed and 6.3 YPC career average in Baltimore make him an ideal fit for McDaniel's scheme (see De'Von Achane/Raheem Mostert comps). A torn MCL/PCL/LCL in 2023 and limited 2025 usage are durability concerns. Solid depth addition — does not create or address a need, but rounds out a suddenly deep RB room.

One-year deal at approximately $3M (per PFR/NBC pattern from prior two identical deals). Perryman started 10 games in 2025 but missed time with a high ankle sprain and was also suspended briefly. At 33, he's a depth/rotational LB and locker room leader. The Chargers have their primary LBs in Daiyan Henley and Junior Colson. This is a veteran minimum-level re-sign that preserves continuity. Does not change the LB need picture.

2-year deal worth up to $7.5M (Bolts From the Blue reports $7.5M; Daily Breeze confirms $7.5M for Phillips). Phillips was the NFL's single-season record holder for Chargers special teams tackles in 2025 with 24, earning Second-Team All-Pro on special teams. This is a pure special teams retention that also provides LB depth. No impact on positional needs.

1-year, $2M re-sign. Leonard is primarily a special teams player who has also flashed as a CB depth piece (picked off a pass in the 2025 playoff loss). He does not address the CB depth vacancy left by St-Juste's departure. CB depth behind Still and Cam Hart remains a need.

Veteran minimum re-sign for the long snapper. Harris suffered a torn Achilles in Week 3 of 2025 but returns as the team's long-term snapper — 93.3% FG make rate over four years with him. Pure special teams, no positional need impact.

Exclusive rights free agent tender — Vidal cannot negotiate with other teams. He had a breakout 2025 season with 643 rushing yards, 3 TDs and three 100-yard games. His retention alongside Hampton and Mitchell gives the Chargers a 3-headed RB room. No need impact — straightforward administrative retention.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: This is the Chargers' single biggest draft need. All three 2025 interior starters are gone: Bozeman retired, Becton cut, Johnson signed with Cleveland (3yr/$49.5M). Biadasz (3yr/$30M) addresses center solidly for 3 years. But both guard spots are effectively unfilled — Cole Strange (2yr/$13M, depth-tier AAV) is a projected starter but a question mark at best as a pass protector. Trevor Penning and Trey Pipkins are depth/swing pieces. With one of the worst OLs in the NFL in 2025 and Justin Herbert's $262M contract to protect, IOL guard is a must-address in rounds 1–3 of the draft. The pick at No. 22 is widely mocked to IOL. Tier escalates to 1 — top priority.

Mack (1yr/$18M, fully guaranteed) keeps a starter but is a 35-year-old on a one-and-done bridge. Tuipulotu (13.5 sacks in 2025, final year of rookie deal) is the true EDGE1 but his extension will cost $25M+/yr. Oweh's departure to Washington (4yr/$100M) removes the team's most explosive pass rusher. The Chargers have only Mack + Tuipulotu as proven edge threats — no depth behind them. EDGE remains a tier 2 need. They need a EDGE3/developmental starter in the draft (rounds 2–4) or via remaining free agency.

Tart re-signed on a 3-year, $30M deal ($20M guaranteed) — this is a real multi-year commitment at mid-tier starter money. He ranked 5th in PFF run defense grade in 2025. Ogbonnia's departure is a minor depth loss. With Tart locked in and Jamaree Caldwell (2024 3rd-round pick) under contract, IDL drops significantly. Depth add via late rounds fine, but not a pressing need.

St-Juste (11th-best coverage grade per PFF, 16 games in 2025) signed with Green Bay (2yr/$10M). The Chargers chose not to match — likely for comp pick reasons. Behind starters Tarheeb Still and Cam Hart, the depth room is nearly empty now. Deane Leonard is a special teams player, not a true depth CB. This creates an escalated need. CB depth/CB3 is now a mid-round draft priority. Could be addressed via remaining free agency or rounds 3–5.

Kolar (3yr/$24.3M, $17M guaranteed) fills a real need and commits 3 years. He pairs with Oronde Gadsden (pass-catcher) to form a functional two-TE set. Kolar as elite blocking TE is ideal for McDaniel/Harbaugh's identity. The need drops a tier but does not fully disappear — the Chargers would still gladly add a premium pass-catching TE in the draft (Kenyon Sadiq at No. 22 has been widely discussed). TE moves from urgent to situational/value play.

Slater and Alt both return from injuries as the starting LT/RT. Pipkins (2yr/$10M) and Penning (1yr/$4.5M) provide solid swing tackle depth. OT is no longer a need — depth is adequate. Low priority in draft.
Henley and Colson are solid returning starters. Perryman re-signed on a 1-year deal for veteran depth/locker room value. Phillips returns for special teams. LB is not a pressing need.
Hampton, Vidal (tendered), Mitchell (2yr/$9.25M) and Ingold (FB) represent one of the most complete backfield rooms in the AFC. RB need fully resolved — do not spend premium draft capital here.

key_additions.headline: Chargers lock up elite run-stopper before market opens — ranked 5th in PFF run defense grade in 2025
Future Hall of Famer returns for Year 5 in LA, anchoring pass rush as Tuli Tuipulotu extension looms
Pro Bowl center fills critical void after Bozeman retirement, graded 15th in PBWR vs. Bozeman's 30th
Highest-paid blocking TE in NFL anchors run-heavy scheme alongside Gadsden as Harbaugh's TE1B
Former first-round pick reunites with OC McDaniel to compete for starting guard role in revamped IOL
4.37-speed speedster adds explosive big-play threat to already deep RB room under Mike McDaniel
Pro Bowl fullback reunites with McDaniel to unlock the Chargers' run-heavy identity in 2026
Versatile swing tackle/guard returns to protect Herbert as insurance behind Slater and Alt

key_losses.headline: Breakout star (7.5 sacks, playoff record 3 sacks) leaves on record deal — biggest loss of LA's offseason
2022 first-round pick and 4-year starting LG walks to Cleveland, deepening the Chargers' IOL crisis
Starting center for two seasons retires, leaving LA with zero centers under contract heading into offseason
Starting RG released after one inconsistent, injury-marred season — three interior starters gone in total
Chargers let elite CB depth walk (11th-best PFF coverage grade) for comp pick formula benefits
